title: Ensure that all Namespaces have Network Policies defined

input_row.title: Ensure that all Namespaces have Network Policies defined
input_row.description: Use network policies to isolate traffic in your cluster network.
input_row.rationale: Running different applications on the same Kubernetes cluster creates a risk of one compromised application attacking a neighboring application. Network segmentation is important to ensure that containers can communicate only with those they are supposed to. A network policy is a specification of how selections of pods are allowed to communicate with each other and other network endpoints. Once there is any Network Policy in a namespace selecting a particular pod, that pod will reject any connections that are not allowed by any Network Policy. Other pods in the namespace that are not selected by any Network Policy will continue to accept all traffic"
input_row.audit: Run the below command and review the NetworkPolicy objects created in the cluster. kubectl get networkpolicy --all-namespaces Ensure that each namespace defined in the cluster has at least one Network Policy.
input_row.remediation: Follow the documentation and create NetworkPolicy objects as you need them.
input_row.impact: Once there is any Network Policy in a namespace selecting a particular pod, that pod will reject any connections that are not allowed by any Network Policy. Other pods in the namespace that are not selected by any Network Policy will continue to accept all traffic"
input_row.default_value: By default, network policies are not created.

step1_initial_assessment:
### AUDIT APPROACH: Automated

### PROGRAM NAME:
aws_ecs_cluster_network_policies_defined

### AUTOMATION DETAILS:
The automated program will check for the existence of Network Policies in each namespace of an Amazon EKS (Elastic Kubernetes Service) cluster. The following AWS API calls and boto3 methods will be utilized:

1. **AWS CLI Commands:**
   - Utilize `kubectl` commands to list Network Policies in all namespaces:
     ```sh
     kubectl get networkpolicy --all-namespaces
     ```

2. **Boto3/SDK Calls:**
   - `boto3` doesn’t have direct support for inspecting Kubernetes resources since it’s tailored for AWS services. Instead, make use of the `kubectl` command which interacts with the Kubernetes API.

3. **Specific Resources to Enumerate:**
   - Connect to the EKS cluster using `kubectl`.
   - Enumerate all namespaces within the cluster and check for the presence of Network Policies.

4. **Configuration Properties/Settings to Validate:**
   - Verify that each namespace in the EKS cluster has at least one Network Policy defined.

5. **Pass/Fail Criteria:**
   - **Pass:** If each namespace contains at least one defined Network Policy.
   - **Fail:** If any namespace does not have at least one defined Network Policy.

6. **Additional Notes:**
   - This check closely resembles manual Kubernetes controls, hence leveraging the `kubectl` directly will allow for an automated inspection of network policies without needing to SSH into any instances. While not a pure AWS API call, managing network policies is essential for securing the containerized applications running in AWS EKS. 

By implementing this automation, organizations can ensure compliance with best practices regarding namespace isolation and network traffic management in their AWS EKS deployments.
